chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: JavaScript, an embarrassing toy language used exclusively to build things it's not supposed to.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: It was created by Brendan Eich in 1995, who was given requirements to build a language like Java, but for f***ers.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: It only took him seven days to execute flawlessly.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: It became an overnight dumpster fire, but shit really hit the fan in 2005,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: when Ryan Dahl made the epic mistake of bringing JavaScript to the server with Node.js.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Just like Alfred Nobel and Dynamite, he would go on to apologize for his invention many years later.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: And it's my fault, and I'm very sorry.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: The language is designed for only one use case, to annoy people with browser pop-ups.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: But today, it's used to build memory-hogging desktop apps with Electron,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: janky mobile apps with React Native, and unreliable APIs with Express.js.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: No matter what you're trying to build, there are hundreds of different game-changing next-gen libraries to get the job done.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: That's because whenever a JavaScript developer encounters a problem,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: it will instinctively create a brand new framework and upload it to GitHub with a bunch of emojis in the readme.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: In fact, there are so many NPM packages that Al Gore is now predicting the Arctic will be ice-free by 2013.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: You have stolen my dreams and my childhood.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Unlike languages people actually like, JavaScript doesn't have a mascot,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: but they are working with David Cronenberg to design one.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: It's a loosely typed language, and by that, I mean completely detached from reality.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: When you add an array to an object, obviously it returns a string that says object.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Object.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Now switch the order of the operands, and predictably, it returns zero.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: If you want a good laugh for yourself, try adding two objects together.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: You'll get object, object, object, object.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Now with that knowledge, what do you think two arrays will equal?

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Obviously, an empty string.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: That makes total sense, because the number zero is equivalent to an empty array,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: and also equivalent to a string of zero.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: And that means the only possible sane conclusion is that a string of zero is also equivalent to an empty array.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: But amazingly, it's not.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Everybody knows you never go full retard.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: It got this way because Netscape did a focus group in the 90s with some homeless web developers they found on the street,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: who thought 2 plus 2 should equal 22.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: And of course, that's why 2 minus 2 equals 0.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: When a language is this bad and this popular at the same time,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: it creates an ecosystem where libraries like isOdd, which does exactly what the name implies,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: can get over 400 down- or no, 400,000 downloads per week.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: The fun thing about packages is that there's multiple module systems, like CommonJS and ESM.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: So you never know if the package you're installing is actually going to work.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Now, if you do manage to build something with JavaScript,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: it'll probably never see the light of day,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: because then you have to learn module bundling with a tool like Webpack.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: And it's just not worth it, man.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: This has been my cathartic release from over 10 years of JavaScript development.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Despite its problems, it's evolved into an awesome language in 2022.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: And I love programming with it.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Today is Thanksgiving, and I'm extremely thankful for JavaScript,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: because it puts food on my family.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: If you want to master the basics,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: all this reverse psychology should motivate you to buy my JavaScript course.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: And if you don't buy it, I won't make any f***ing money.

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: Thanks for watching, and I will see you in the next one,

chunks.title: JavaScript
chunks.text: assuming I'm not entirely cancelled after this video.
